revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:教育令]]
{{Nihon no hōrei |題名 = Kyōikurei (教育令) |番号 = {{Nihon no hōrei/hōrei bangō|Q117378300|qq=}} |通称 = {{Nihon no hōrei/tanshuku-mei|Q117378300|qq=}} |効力 = Abolished |種類 = [[Education Law]] |成立 = {{Nihon no hōrei/seiritsu-nichi/nen tsuki nichi|Q117378300|qq=}} |公布 = {{Nihon no hōrei/kōfu-nichi/nen tsuki nichi|Q117378300|qq=}} |施行 = {{Nihon no hōrei/shikō-nichi/nen tsuki nichi|Q117378300|qq=}} |所管 = |内容 = |関連 = |リンク = [{{NDLDC|787966/159|qq=}} Hōrei Zensho Meiji 18 Jōkan] |ウィキソース = }} '''Kyōikurei''' (教育令, Meiji 12, September 29, Daijōkan Proclamation No. 40)<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/787959/67 |title=Hōrei Zensho Meiji 12 |accessdate=2022-03-12 |pages=75-78 |publisher=[[National Diet Library]]|qq=}}</ref> replaced the [[Gakusei]] (Meiji 5, Daijōkan Proclamation No. 214), promulgated in {{ill|1872|en|1872|qid=Q7753|lt=1872|1=1872}} (Meiji 5), as an educational [[Daijōkan Proclamation]]. This article also covers its two amendments: the Second Kyōikurei (Meiji 13, December 28, Daijōkan Proclamation No. 59)<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/787960/202 |title=Hōrei Zensho Meiji 13 |accessdate=2022-03-12 |publisher=[[National Diet Library]] |pages=325-329|qq=}}</ref> and the Third Kyōikurei (Meiji 18, August 12, Daijōkan Proclamation No. 23)<ref>{{Cite web|和書|url=https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/787966/159 |title=Hōrei Zensho Meiji 18 Jōkan |accessdate=2022-03-12 |publisher=[[National Diet Library]] |pages=49-52|qq=}}</ref>. == Overview == The Kyōikurei reduced the authority granted to local officials (prefectural governors) and abolished the district school system. While it relaxed school attendance requirements to reflect the burden on private schools and citizens, it faced strong criticism, leading to two amendments in {{ill|1880|en|1880|qid=Q7806|lt=1880|1=1880}} (Meiji 13) and {{ill|1885|en|1885|qid=Q7821|lt=1885|1=1885}} (Meiji 18). It was abolished in {{ill|1886|en|1886|qid=Q7822|lt=1886|1=1886}} (Meiji 19) following the issuance of a series of [[Gakkōrei]] (School Ordinances) under the first [[:en:Minister of Education|Minister of Education]], {{ill|Mori Arinori|en|Mori Arinori|qid=Q1194472|lt=Mori Arinori|1=Mori Arinori}}. == First Kyōikurei == ;Date of Promulgation :September 29, 1879 (Meiji 12) ;Background of Promulgation :The [[Gakusei]], modeled after Western education systems, proved challenging to implement uniformly across Japan due to its centralized nature, economic burdens on local areas, and cultural differences. The Ministry of Education began reforming the Gakusei in {{ill|1877|en|1877|qid=Q7773|lt=1877|1=1877}} (Meiji 10), culminating in the Kyōikurei. ;Key Features :* Decentralized educational authority, granting significant autonomy to local regions. :* Composed of 47 articles, simplified compared to the Gakusei. :* Divided schools into elementary, middle, university, teacher training, specialized, and other categories. :* Focused on improving elementary education. :* Abolished the district school system, using towns and villages as the basis for school establishment. :* Introduced local election of "educational commissioners" to manage school affairs. :* Relaxed compulsory education, requiring a minimum of 16 months during the school age period. :* Allowed private elementary schools to substitute public ones. :* Permitted itinerant teaching methods in economically disadvantaged areas. == Second Kyōikurei == ;Date of Promulgation :December 28, 1880 (Meiji 13) ;Background of Amendment :The decentralization under the First Kyōikurei, often called the "Free Education Ordinance," led to reduced school attendance and financial difficulties for schools. These issues prompted a shift back toward centralized control. ;Key Features :* Introduced state control as the basic policy, reinforcing oversight by the Ministry of Education. :* Expanded school types to include agricultural, commercial, and industrial schools. :* Required a11roval from the Ministry for establishing or abolishing public schools. :* Clarified compulsory education as three years, with the possibility of extension under specific conditions. :* Strengthened prefectural responsibilities for teacher training. == Third Kyōikurei == ;Date of Promulgation :August 12, 1885 (Meiji 18) ;Background of Amendment :Despite progress in education, economic downturns and the cessation of government subsidies for schools created challenges. The Third Kyōikurei aimed to address these financial issues. ;Key Features :* Focused on reducing education costs in response to economic difficulties. :* Simplified provisions to 31 articles. :* Allowed for "simple educational facilities" called elementary teaching stations. :* Shifted local educational responsibilities to town and village heads. == References == {{Reflist|qq=}}
== See Also == {{Wikisource|Kyōikurei|qq=}} * [[Gakkōrei]] * [[Shōgakkōrei]] * [[Chūgakkōrei]] * [[Shihan Gakkōrei]] * [[Teikoku Daigakurei]] * [[Kenrei#Japan's Kenrei]] == External Links == * [https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317552.htm Education Ordinances Centennial History - Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science, and Technology] {{Japanese Education History|qq=}} {{Gakkōrei|qq=}}
{{translated page|ja|教育令|version=103677144|comment=Imported full ja history}}
[[ja:教育令]]
